transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello Issac, I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been thinking a lot about my family lately. There’s been some stuff going on with my dad’s health, and it’s just been weighing on me. I don’t know, it feels like every time my mom calls, it’s something heavy. I just... I feel a bit overwhelmed.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like your dad's health issues and the conversations with your mom have been really heavy on your mind. Feeling overwhelmed in situations like this is completely understandable. Can you share more about what specifically has been weighing on you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... when my mom calls, I can’t help but think that it’s always bad news. I mean, it’s like she only reaches out when something’s wrong. And then there’s this feeling that my dad doesn’t really share what’s going on with me. It makes me feel kind of distant from them, like I’m not really part of the conversation. I guess I just feel anxious about it all, you know? Like, what if something really serious happens? It’s hard to talk about these things.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a mix of anxiety and distance when it comes to your family, especially regarding your dad's health. It must be tough to feel like your mom's calls are often bringing bad news, and that you’re not fully included in what’s happening. That sense of uncertainty about the future can be really overwhelming. How do you usually cope with those feelings when they come up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I tend to just... disconnect a bit. Like, when I’m on the phone with my mom, I find myself zoning out or just... watching TV instead. It’s like I’m there, but not really present. I don’t know, it feels easier to just numb out than to dive into those heavy conversations. I think I’m scared of what might come up if I really engage. So, I just kind of... avoid it, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that you would want to disconnect when things feel too heavy. Numbing out can feel like a way to protect yourself from the emotional weight of those conversations. It sounds like there's a fear of what might come up if you engage more deeply, which is completely valid. What do you think it is that you’re most afraid of facing in those conversations?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s... just the fear of really facing the reality of the situation. Like, what if things are worse than I imagine? Or what if I can’t handle it? I mean, I want to be there for my parents, but it feels like there’s this wall between us. I worry that if I dig deeper, I might find out things I’m not ready to hear. It’s easier to just keep things light, even if it feels kind of empty.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That fear of confronting a reality that might be more difficult than you expect is really understandable. It sounds like you’re caught between wanting to support your parents and feeling the weight of that potential reality, which can create a wall between you. Keeping things light might feel safer, but it also leaves you feeling empty. How do you feel about the idea of finding a balance between being there for them and protecting yourself from that heaviness?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I... I think that balance sounds nice in theory, but I’m not sure how to actually do it. I mean, part of me wants to be there for them, but then there's this other part that just wants to retreat and avoid any real talk. It feels like I’m stuck in this loop where I want to connect, but I also don’t want to feel overwhelmed. I guess I’m just not sure how to approach it without feeling like I’m taking on too much.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling really torn between the desire to connect with your parents and the instinct to protect yourself from feeling overwhelmed. That loop of wanting to be there but also wanting to retreat can be really frustrating. It’s okay to feel unsure about how to approach this. What do you think might help you take small steps toward that connection without feeling like you’re taking on too much at once?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe... starting with smaller conversations could help? Like, instead of diving into the heavy stuff right away, I could just check in on them, see how they’re doing without jumping straight to the health issues. I think that might feel a bit less daunting. But even that feels a little scary, like what if they start talking about something serious? I guess it’s just hard to know where to draw the line.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Starting with smaller conversations sounds like a thoughtful approach, and it makes sense that it would feel less daunting. It’s natural to feel apprehensive about where those conversations might lead, especially if you're worried about them turning serious. Finding that line can be tricky. What do you think might help you feel more comfortable if the conversation does shift to something heavier?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I suppose... having a plan in mind could help? Like, maybe if I think about some questions or topics I can bring up if it starts to get heavy, it might make me feel a bit more prepared. And maybe reminding myself that it’s okay to set boundaries if I need to take a step back. I just don’t want to feel overwhelmed, but I also don’t want to shut them out completely. It’s just... finding that balance feels tough.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Having a plan and some questions in mind sounds like a great way to feel more prepared for those conversations. It’s also really important to remind yourself that setting boundaries is okay, especially when you’re trying to navigate such a complex situation. It’s clear that you care deeply about your parents and want to be there for them, while also taking care of yourself. What do you think would be the first step you’d like to take in trying out this approach?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe the first step could be just reaching out to my mom for a casual chat. I could ask her how she’s been doing, maybe share something light from my day. Just keeping it simple at first. If it starts to get heavy, I can remind myself that it’s okay to steer it back to something lighter if I need to. It feels a bit scary, but I think it’s worth a try.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful and manageable first step. Keeping it simple and light while giving yourself permission to steer the conversation if it gets heavy is a great way to approach it. It’s completely normal to feel a bit scared, but it also sounds like you’re ready to give it a try. How do you feel about setting a time to reach out to her?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think setting a time could help me feel more committed to it. Maybe I could call her this weekend? That gives me a little time to gather my thoughts and feel a bit more prepared. I just hope it goes well. I really want to connect, but I guess I’m still a bit nervous about how it’ll play out.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Calling her this weekend sounds like a solid plan, and giving yourself that time to prepare can help ease some of the nerves. It’s completely natural to feel a bit anxious about how it will go, especially when you care about connecting with her. Remember, it’s okay to take it at your own pace and adjust as needed during the conversation. What do you think might help you manage those nerves as the call approaches?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe taking some time to breathe and remind myself that it’s just a conversation could help. I could also jot down a few things I want to talk about or questions I might ask, just to have something to fall back on if I start feeling anxious. Maybe even talking it out with someone else before the call could help me feel a bit more grounded. I just want to make sure I’m in a good headspace before I reach out.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like excellent strategies for managing your nerves. Taking time to breathe, jotting down thoughts, and even talking it out with someone else can all help you feel more grounded and prepared. It’s great that you’re prioritizing your own headspace before reaching out. How do you feel about sharing your plan with someone close to you? Do you think that would help you feel more supported?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I think sharing my plan with someone close could be really helpful. It might make me feel less alone in this, and they could offer some encouragement or even share their own experiences. I have a friend I trust, so maybe I’ll talk to them about it. Just having that support might help ease some of the pressure I’m feeling.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a wonderful idea. Sharing your plan with a trusted friend can definitely provide you with support and encouragement, and it can help you feel less alone in this process. It’s great that you have someone you can turn to. How do you feel about reaching out to them soon to discuss your plan?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ll try to reach out to them soon, maybe later today. It feels good to have a plan in place and to know I can talk to someone about it. I think just sharing my thoughts and getting their perspective might help me feel more confident going into the call with my mom. It’s nice to know I’m not navigating this alone.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I'm really glad to hear that you’re feeling good about your plan and the support you have. Sharing your thoughts with your friend can definitely help build your confidence and make the process feel a bit lighter. It’s important to have that sense of connection, especially when navigating challenging situations. As you prepare for your call with your mom, remember to be gentle with yourself. Is there anything else on your mind today that you’d like to explore?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ve shared a lot already, and it feels good to have a plan in place. I guess I just want to make sure I don’t lose sight of how I’m feeling while I’m trying to connect with my parents. It’s easy to get caught up in their stuff and forget about my own emotions. Maybe just keeping that in mind will help me stay grounded. Thank you for talking through this with me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s great that you’re aware of the importance of staying in touch with your own feelings while connecting with your parents. That self-awareness will definitely help you navigate those conversations more effectively. Remember, it’s okay to prioritize your emotions too. I’m glad we could talk this through together, and I appreciate your openness. If you ever want to revisit this or explore anything else, I’m here for you. Wishing you the best with your call this weekend!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate that. It feels good to have talked things through and to have a plan. I’ll definitely keep in mind what we discussed as I prepare for the call. I might reach out again if I need to talk more. Thanks for your support!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome! I’m glad to hear that you found our conversation helpful and that you have a plan in place. Feel free to reach out anytime you want to talk more or if you need support. Take care of yourself, and I wish you all the best with your call. You’ve got this!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! That means a lot. I’ll take care, and I’ll keep you updated if I need to talk again. I appreciate your support and understanding. Have a great day!
